steps.title: Identify the subject
steps.content:
The subject is Mathematician Grigori Perelman — singular.

The phrase sometimes in conjunction with mathematicians Richard S.

Hamilton and Shing-Tung Yau is a parenthetical interrupter set off by commas; it doesn't change the subject. (Phrases beginning with with, along with, etc., do not affect subject-verb agreement.)

steps.title: Apply the rule
steps.content:
Identify the grammar issue: subject-verb agreement. The subject is "Mathematician Grigori Perelman" — singular. The phrase "sometimes in conjunction with mathematicians Richard S. Hamilton and Shing-Tung Yau" is a parenthetical set off by commas; it does not change the subject. Rule: prepositional phrases beginning with "in conjunction with," "along with," etc. do not make a singular subject plural.
  • Choice A — "are": Plural present — treats the parenthetical as adding to the subject, which it does not. ✗
  • Choice B — "are being": Plural progressive — same number error, plus progressive aspect is inappropriate for a long-established attribution. ✗
  • Choice C — "have been": Plural perfect — wrong number; also implies the credit is no longer current, contradicting the passage's present context. ✗
  • Choice D — "is": Singular simple present. Correctly agrees with the singular subject "Perelman." ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
